title: Prompt-Driven Development: What Happens When You Let AI Lead the Build
author: Oshri Mahlev
text:
Prompt-Driven Development: What Happens When You Let AI Lead the Build
Over the past few months, I’ve been building a side project with a simple idea and a big question: Can I build a real, valuable product — solo — using mostly AI tools?
Not to raise funding. Not to impress investors. Just to explore, learn, and test what’s possible with the tools we have today.
🔍 The Beginning: From Curiosity to Structure
Before I wrote a single line of code, I started with structure.
I set up a well-organized Notion workspace structured like a real internal knowledge base — not a loose document, but a living system of folders and docs:
- 📄 Product Overview — a high-level snapshot I could always return to
- 🔍 Market & Competitor Research — screenshots, pricing models, SEO patterns
- 🧠 Core Features & Insights — distilled conclusions from my research
- 💸 Cost & Infrastructure — early estimates for building + scaling
- 📦 MVP Scope — trimmed-down feature list I could build solo
- 🚀 Go-to-Market Strategy — early thoughts on how to launch
- 🤖 LLM Prompt Design — organized experiments and real use-case prompts
- 🧱 Technical Architecture — schema, stack, limitations
- ✍️ Content & Copywriting — messaging, value props, and positioning ideas
This Notion structure helped me move faster later — I wasn’t starting from scratch when I got to the build phase.
That research became the foundation for everything that followed.
🧱 Phase 1: Research → Design → Code
What I did
- Used ChatGPT to explore the problem space and audience needs
- Summarized that into a product brief
- Sent the brief to UXPilot to generate fully designed flows
- Implemented them in Cursor
What worked
- I moved fast.
- I had clear UI to build against.
What didn’t work
- The frontend was pixel-perfect but rigid.
- The backend? A mess.
- Code wasn’t maintainable or reusable. It just mimicked a single use-case.
How I used the Notion project
I pulled specific UI needs directly from the research (e.g., which metrics matter to the user). This helped shape what each screen should do — but I didn’t abstract anything beyond that.
⚙️ Phase 2: Backend-First with Technical Prompting
What I did
- Designed a full ERD with relationships and constraints
- Defined CRUD endpoints
- Used ChatGPT to generate highly technical prompts for Cursor to implement
Example Prompt
## Code Style and Structure
- Use functional components and React Hooks
- Follow file structure: /components, /pages, /hooks, /lib, /context, /utils
- Favor named exports, modularity, and simplicity## TypeScript
- Use TypeScript or make code easily upgradable
- Prefer interfaces over types, avoid enums## Styling
- Use Tailwind CSS, mobile-first, semantic HTML## Data Layer
- Use Supabase client only (no custom API or backend)
- Enforce RLS and direct table access## Performance
- Lazy load components, optimize Web Vitals, use dynamic imports## Collaboration Rules
- Ask before adding routes/features or external libraries
- Explain how to manually test or verify any change
What didn’t work
- GPT prompts became too long and fragile
- Everything lived in one massive chat session
- Iteration became painful. Small changes broke the flow
How I used the Notion project
I built the ERD based on early drafts in the data_model
folder. The structured thinking helped—but the execution got trapped inside a monolithic conversation.
🧠 Phase 3: Memory Bank + Cursor Rules + GPT as Planner
What I did
- Created Cursor Rules to define best practices and patterns
- Built a Memory Bank with: Common modal structure, Table and layout patterns, Reusable components
- Used ChatGPT only for high-level planning — not coding
Example Prompt:
🎯 Task: Redesign the Prompt Page (`[redacted]/prompts`) as a multi-tab dashboard🧠 Purpose:
This is one of the most critical pages in the product — where users evaluate how their prompts perform across LLMs in terms of key performance indicators and competitive visibility.👤 Target User:
- Power users managing multiple prompts per brand
- Typically from agencies or SEO teams📦 Tables to Use:🗂 Tabs to Implement:
1. Summary
2. Competitors
🧪 UX Notes:
- Use existing components from Memory Bank
- Consistent tab layout, filters, loading states⚙️ Next:
Review Memory Bank → Propose layout + data-fetching strategy → Implement
What worked
- Cursor had structure and memory
- Code became reusable and consistent
- GPT stopped over-controlling, and started guiding
How I used the Notion project
Earlier research about user needs, key metrics, and usage patterns was converted into Memory Bank entries, which gave Cursor meaningful context — without hardcoding logic.
🔮 Phase 4 (Coming Soon): Dev Mind
The next evolution: take all this scattered knowledge — ERDs, prompts, code rules, planning docs — and move it into a real system:
A structured, modular, queryable knowledge layer for product context
What I’m building:
Dev Mind — a context engine for agentic development.
- Replaces ad-hoc Notion/GPT/doc combos
- Provides shared memory for humans + agents
- Makes project knowledge persistent, composable, and reusable
Stay tuned.
